skill.name: enact-hello-rust
skill.description: A minimal Enact skill that compiles and runs a Rust greeting program.
skill.summary: Compile and run a small Rust greeting example with Enact.
skill.author: EnactProtocol

security_audit.summary: The static weak-cryptography findings at SKILL.md lines 5 and 14 are false positives because those lines contain description text only. The reported shell execution at SKILL.md lines 33-35 is also a usage example, but the skill does define build and runtime commands. The runtime command interpolates user input, so publication should include a warning or verification of safe argument handling.

medium_findings.title: User Input Interpolated Into Runtime Command
medium_findings.description: The runtime command passes the name input through the command template. This is expected for the greeting tool, but safe publication should verify that Enact passes the value as an argument and not through shell parsing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 24 clearly references the name input in a command template. I did not find evidence of malicious intent, and exploitability depends on Enact command execution semantics.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Finding
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography at two metadata description lines. Those lines only describe the skill and input field, and no cryptographic algorithm or crypto API appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain YAML description strings. There is no evidence of hashing, encryption, or any cryptographic operation at those locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive Shell Execution in Usage Example
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged the fenced bash example as shell execution. The cited lines document how to run the skill and are not executed automatically by the skill definition.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern appears inside a Markdown code fence under the Usage heading. It is documentation, although it still shows that users run the skill through an external CLI.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Build and Runtime Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill builds Rust source with rustc and runs the compiled binary. This is normal for the skill purpose, but it requires execution privileges in the Enact runtime.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The build and command fields are explicit in the skill metadata. They are legitimate for this example but remain relevant execution-risk evidence.

content.value_statement: Quick Rust examples help developers verify Enact skill execution. This skill compiles a small Rust program and returns a greeting from a name input.
actual_capabilities: Accepts a name input and uses World as the default value.
Builds hello.rs with rustc inside a Rust slim image.
Runs the compiled greeting binary through the Enact command field.
Prints a simple Rust greeting for the provided name.
Includes an Enact CLI usage example for local testing.
limitations: It only produces a greeting and does not perform broader Rust project tasks.
It depends on Enact runtime support for container build and command execution.
It does not validate or sanitize the name beyond passing it to the command.
It is an example skill, not a full application template.

use_cases.title: Verify Enact Setup
use_cases.description: Run a minimal Rust skill to confirm local Enact execution works.

use_cases.title: Teach Skill Basics
use_cases.description: Show how a skill defines input, output, build, and command fields.

use_cases.title: Start a Rust Skill
use_cases.description: Use the small example as a starting point for a simple Rust command skill.

prompt_templates.title: Run the Example
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the enact-hello-rust skill with the name Alice and show the greeting result.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how this Rust Enact skill uses its input schema, build command, runtime command, and output.

prompt_templates.title: Customize the Greeting
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the greeting behavior to include a short welcome phrase while keeping the same input and output shape.

prompt_templates.title: Review Command Safety
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the command template for user input handling and recommend a safer argument passing approach if needed.

output_examples.output: The skill returns a greeting for Alice from the Rust program.
The skill uses the default name and returns a greeting for World.

output_examples.input: A developer asks how the example works.
output: Explains the build and runtime commands.
Notes the single name input and greeting output.

best_practices: Use simple names when testing the command template.
Review runtime argument handling before adapting this example for untrusted input.
Keep the input and output schema aligned with the Rust program behavior.
anti_patterns: Do not treat this example as a full Rust project generator.
Do not pass untrusted shell metacharacters without checking runtime escaping.
Do not add unrelated filesystem or network access to a greeting example.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It compiles a small Rust program and runs it to print a greeting for a provided name.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it require Rust?
faq.answer: The skill uses a Rust container image and builds the included hello.rs file with rustc.

faq.question: Can it greet different names?
faq.answer: Yes. It accepts a name input and defaults to World when no name is provided.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It appears publishable with a medium warning because it executes commands and interpolates input into the runtime command.

faq.question: Is this a production Rust template?
faq.answer: No. It is a minimal example for learning and testing Enact skill execution.
